subtitle: Safeguarding Student Data Privacy
purpose: A practical tool for evaluating whether an educational technology solution meets the necessary standards for protecting student data across U.S. federal, state, and international regulatory environments.
regulationAware: FERPA, COPPA, CIPA, PPRA, SCOPE Act (Texas), SOPIPA-style state laws, NY Ed Law 2-d, IL SOPPA, CCPA/CPRA, GDPR, and the Student Data Privacy Consortium NDPA.

scores.label: Does not meet expectations
scores.description: Silent on the topic, vague, or actively contradicts best practice.

scores.label: Partially meets
scores.description: Addresses the topic but leaves material gaps, lacks specifics, or has carve-outs that weaken the commitment.

scores.label: Fully meets
scores.description: Clear, specific, time-bound, regulation-aware, and operationally verifiable.

interpretation.label: Approve
interpretation.description: Document the score and re-audit annually.

interpretation.label: Approve with conditions
interpretation.description: Require a signed district data privacy addendum that closes the gaps below a 2 on any category.

interpretation.label: Hold
interpretation.description: Request remediation; reassess after the vendor revises documentation or product behavior.

interpretation.label: Do not approve for student use
interpretation.description: Does not meet minimum privacy expectations.

categories.name: Parental Rights and Access
categories.lookForShort: Access, correction, export, deletion, response timelines, working intake channel.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: FERPA (34 CFR §99), COPPA (16 CFR §312), state student privacy laws (NY Ed Law 2-d, SCOPE Act §509.052, IL SOPPA, CA SOPIPA), GDPR Articles 12–22.
whatToLookFor: Named procedure for parents and eligible students 18+ to request access, correction, export, and deletion of student records.
Stated response time windows — best practice: acknowledge within 10 school days, fulfill within 30 calendar days.
A working intake mechanism such as a web form, dedicated email, or parent portal — not just a generic contact address.
For COPPA-covered students under 13: explicit verifiable parental consent mechanism, or a clear FERPA school official delegation of consent.

levels.0: No mention of parental rights, or rights are conditional on payment / extra agreement.
levels.1: Parental rights are listed but lack timeframes, lack an intake mechanism, or apply only to some accounts.
levels.2: Specific rights enumerated, with named timeframes, working intake channel, and explicit handling for COPPA-covered minors and eligible students.

categories.name: Retention and Deletion
categories.lookForShort: Retention schedule, return/destruction on termination, backups, user-initiated deletion.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: FERPA, COPPA, SCOPE Act, NY Ed Law 2-d, GDPR Article 17, CCPA §1798.105.
whatToLookFor: Concrete retention schedule by data type, not just 'until you ask us to delete it.'
Return-and-destruction-on-termination clause with a clear deadline.
Backup handling and explicit deletion propagation through backup cycles.
User-initiated deletion path with confirmation.
De-identification or aggregation before any retention beyond the educational purpose.

levels.0: No retention schedule; data kept indefinitely; deletion is best-effort or requires unlocking a paid tier.
levels.1: Some retention windows stated; deletion possible but backups, exports, or third-party copies not addressed.
levels.2: Per-data-type retention schedule, named termination process with explicit active/backup deadlines, user-initiated deletion, and de-identification rules for any post-termination retention.

categories.name: Opt-Out Options
categories.lookForShort: No targeted ads, no profiling, no AI/ML training on student data, per-feature opt-outs.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: COPPA, CCPA/CPRA Do Not Sell or Share, GDPR Article 21, PPRA.
whatToLookFor: Explicit prohibition on targeted advertising, behavioral profiling, and cross-context tracking of students.
Explicit prohibition on AI / ML model training with student data.
Opt-out for optional features that increase data collection.
Browser-only or local-only mode, or a clear statement that no opt-out is needed because no data is collected by default.
Honors Global Privacy Control and Do Not Track where applicable.

levels.0: No opt-out provided, or opt-out only by terminating the account. Advertising / AI training silent or permitted.
levels.1: Some opt-outs exist but advertising, profiling, or model training are not explicitly prohibited; modern signals not honored.
levels.2: Targeted advertising, profiling, sale, and AI/ML training are categorically prohibited for student data; per-feature opt-outs exist; modern signals honored where applicable.

categories.name: Transparency
categories.lookForShort: Data inventory, hosting/data residency, named subprocessors, feature boundaries, update dates.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: GDPR Articles 13–14, CCPA §1798.100, FERPA annual notification, state data inventory laws including NY Ed Law 2-d.
whatToLookFor: Definition of student personal information that meets or exceeds the strictest applicable law's definition.
Comprehensive data inventory: what is collected, how, by which subsystem, and whether it is persisted, transmitted, or only in browser memory.
Hosting / data residency disclosure including country, region, provider, and backup locations.
Named subprocessors with their roles and jurisdictions.
Per-widget or per-feature security boundary if the product is modular.
Visible Last updated date and a changelog or notification mechanism for material changes.

levels.0: Vague 'we collect what we need' language; no data inventory; no hosting info; no subprocessors named.
levels.1: Some data categories listed, but vague about subprocessors, hosting, or per-feature differences; changelog absent.
levels.2: Full data inventory, named subprocessors with jurisdictions, hosting location stated, per-feature security boundary, dated revisions, and a notification path for material changes.

categories.name: Encryption and Security
categories.lookForShort: TLS in transit, encryption at rest, backup protection, breach timeline, access controls.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: FERPA reasonable methods, COPPA §312.8, HIPAA where health data is implicated, state breach notification laws, NIST SP 800-171 / 800-53, NDPA Exhibit E.
whatToLookFor: TLS in transit everywhere: HTTPS, HSTS, modern cipher suites.
Encryption at rest for databases, object storage, file uploads, audio recordings, and backups.
Content security policy and modern hardening headers.
Breach notification timeline named in the policy.
Access control model, including who can see student data and what audit logging exists.
For self-hosted products: guidance on secrets management, secure defaults, and patch cadence.

levels.0: No mention of encryption, or only HTTPS without at-rest commitment; no breach timeline; no documented access controls.
levels.1: TLS confirmed; some at-rest encryption; breach process exists but timeline is vague; access controls undocumented.
levels.2: TLS + at-rest including backups, named breach notification window, documented access-control model with audit logs, modern web hardening, and self-host security guidance if applicable.

categories.name: Consent and Age Restrictions
categories.lookForShort: Age bands, COPPA / FERPA pathways, parent visibility, age-appropriate design.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: COPPA, SCOPE Act (Texas), GDPR Article 8, CCPA under-16 sale opt-in, ISO/IEC 27701, age-appropriate design laws, CIPA.
whatToLookFor: Explicit age band model and how each band's data is treated differently.
FERPA school-official consent delegation clearly stated when school-controlled use substitutes for direct parental consent.
Minors-by-default protections, parental visibility into account actions, restrictions on advertising and addictive design patterns.
Verifiable parental consent mechanism for COPPA-covered direct use outside a school-authorized context.
Age-appropriate UX: default privacy, no dark patterns, accessible consent prompts.

levels.0: One-size-fits-all consent; no age-band treatment; relies on user-asserted age with no district override.
levels.1: COPPA addressed; FERPA school-official path exists; older-minor protections weak or unstated; no SCOPE / state-specific posture.
levels.2: Explicit age bands with differentiated processing, both COPPA VPC and FERPA school-official paths documented, SCOPE-Act / state-of-deployment posture stated, age-appropriate design defaults enforced.

categories.name: Third-party Management
categories.lookForShort: Live subprocessor list, roles and jurisdictions, 30-day notice, scope-of-processing limits.
categories.regulatoryAnchors: FERPA school official doctrine, COPPA §312.4(d)(1), GDPR Articles 28–29, NDPA Exhibit H, SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria.
whatToLookFor: Current subprocessor list with company name, country, and function.
Change-notification window for subprocessor additions, ideally 30 days with district objection path.
Flow-down requirements binding subprocessors to the same data protection standards.
Cross-border transfer mechanism where applicable.
No data sale or sharing for the subprocessor's own purposes.
If SSO / OAuth is supported, requested scopes and what is stored after authentication.

levels.0: Subprocessors not named; 'we may use third parties' boilerplate; no change notice; cross-border transfers undocumented.
levels.1: Some major subprocessors listed but list is not comprehensive, dated, or kept current; change notice exists but no objection path.
levels.2: Live subprocessor list with named jurisdiction and role, 30-day change notice with objection path, flow-down requirements stated, named cross-border transfer mechanism, and clear scope-of-processing limits.
